sentences.text: But I want to tell you-- before I went to the Red Army, I was call up the Polish army.
I have to go in a different place, a different little town, where the Polish army officers was there.
As I was coming there the Polish army, I saw the bloody anti-Semite, like was before the war.
Make me sick.
And I hear when they call up somebody to go to the office to fix the papers, they call two names, like Jim, John, something like that, two names.
When they call my name, they call only Ganc, not Mojsze.
Because Mojsze, you know Mojsze what was?
Mojsze the man which he brought in the, the Ten Commandment.
They don't want to mention this name.
They called Ganc.
I went to the office, the men.
They asked me on Polish language, what's your name?
I said, Mojsze Ganc.
He said, what?
Mojsze Ganc.
Don't want it.
Don't need it.
Sent me home.
Of course, I got a Jew.
A few weeks later, in June, I got a Yiddish name.
A few weeks later, I call again.
The same thing.
But after that, they called me the Red Army.
The Red Army, as I told you before, I was two weeks, only two weeks training how to shoot a rife, how to shoot a machine gun on the front.
I was on the front in Ukraine, and Russia, White Russia.
Till 1944, our army was in Poland.
Our regiment was, was in a little forest not far from the town where I used to be-- I met Leja and she was coming from, from, from where she was going from [?
Pole, ?] they may meet.
In little town, I were there.
I, I, I, I was in a forest.
I was there in a forest.
And this forest-- this little town was not far from Chelm, maybe 20, 25, or-- [? either ?] 20 kilometers.
Our regiment went into Chelm and kicked out the Germans.
So I told-- when I was in Chelm-- I told my, my, my, my [NON-ENGLISH], my regiment member, [? red, ?] in this town, I was born in 1914.
And I was living in this town till 1939.
And if it's possible, I would be able to go and look if I will find somebody.
All right.
He went with me, because we got only one hour or two hours time, because we have to go back.
He went with me.
I can find nowhere no Jewish people. 18,000 people, Jewish people, were living.
No one.
Anyway, we arrived at the door.
And I speak to him in Polish.
They saw a Russian soldier with speak Polish, they shut the door.
They didn't want to speak to me.
They didn't want to speak to me.
And I can't find.
What can I find, one, one Jew even.
I saw a man walking.
I recognized the man.
He was a shoe repair man.
This man was working next door to me was a shoe repair man.
And he was working and this man-- by this man.
I recognized him.
I called him out by the name.
I stopped him.
And I told him, I remember you.
You was working by this man [?
Stippel. ?] The boss name was [?
Stippel. ?] You remember, you was working with him.
As he saw I, I remember him.
And I tell him all the, all the [INAUDIBLE], everything.
I ask him, any Jewish people live here, any left?
He told me, yes, [POLISH] Street number 39, a few Jewish people, they were there.
I with [INAUDIBLE] went over there.
I opened the door.
I recognized about 8 or 10 Yiddish people.
That was a good tailor, a good shoemaker, a good carpenter, a good plumber, a good shoemaker, a dressmaker.
They was working for the, for the German, for the [NON-ENGLISH], for the leader from the, from the-- privately they was working.
And they kept them to work for them, but kept them so long till they would be able a chance to kill them.
But we went so fast, so quick, they didn't have time but left them, left alive.
And they, they, left them alive.
I was crying.
And they was crying.
I tell them my name.
They knew me.
We, we know each other.
I went back to my--
Nothing, nothing left.
Nothing left.
The bomb-- they, they, they, destroyed.
Everything, they destroyed.
We went back to this place [? we was, ?] the regiment we was in their forest.
At that time, was raining two days and three nights, no stop at that time.
The [? trench ?] was full of water.
Our clothes was wet like we walk out from a beach.
And I have to carry my army bag with, with the bag with ammunition, with my rifle, with my gas mask, with my bayonet.
Everything, I carry.
And that night, I have be on guard from 10 to 12 o'clock in the nighttime.
I was feeling so bad, so-- I was really fighting.
I can't stand it.
And suddenly, the moon comes out.
The rain stopped, suddenly.
I told myself, if I will be able to find a place where can sit down, I would have a rest for few minutes, a few minutes.
As the moon was coming out, I saw maybe a few meters from me, a tree was cutted off.
The tree was cut off not to the ground, cut off a little bit higher to be able to sit there.
I went over there.
And I sit down.
And as you know, what strict regulations is in the army, you [INAUDIBLE].
You're not allowed to do that.
You're not allowed to do that.
And I sit down.
And I fall asleep.
I mention, ?] I fall asleep.
What I done?
I don't know how long I was sleeping.
But I think that that's what happened.
[NON-ENGLISH], my, the man from, from, from the regiment, when the rain stopped, the moon was coming out.
He decided to go have a look around if everything is in order, the guard is in order, everything is in order.
As he saw, the moon was shining to the bayonet.
And the bayonet doesn't move.
That's mean somebody is sleeping or something like that.
And he started come to me, started come to me.
And as he was already a few meters from me, suddenly, I wake up.
I don't know what's hap-- I wake up.
And I open my eyes.
I saw [NON-ENGLISH].
He stretch his arm.
He want grab my rifle.
If he would grab my rifle, he would took the rifle to headquarters, I get shot sleeping on the-- sleeping on guard.
But I wake up.
I don't know what's happened.
And I move away from this tree.
I move away.
And he comes to me closer.
He called me, Ganc.
You sleep!
He know my name, because I was his adjutant a few weeks before.
I was his adjutant.
He know me before.
He know me.
I said, I'm not sleeping.
He said, you asleep.
He said, no sleeping.
You're not sleeping now, but you were sleeping before.
And he start to deliver a speech, a moral speech.
Ganc, he told me, you know what responsibility you got here.
The Germans, they only are two meters or three meters from here.
They was-- that's [INAUDIBLE] the Germans.
We was in the forest.
They was outside the forest.
If they will come, they will kill you.
They will kill us.
They will take away all the, all the ammunition.
He speaks a, a moral speech.
And I told myself, if [INAUDIBLE] [NON-ENGLISH] and I will [? disappear, ?] I would be happy not to listen to his speech.
After he finished speech about few minutes, he come close to me.
He put his arm on my shoulder.
He tell me, Ganc.
[SPEAKING RUSSIAN].
On Russian, that mean pity.
I did not report.
You [SPEAKING RUSSIAN], not report.
Then after we went kick out the Germans from [NON-ENGLISH], we went further.
Majdanek was already free.
We went further.
We come to the river, a town named [?
Pulawy. ?] This town is the river Wisla, Vistula, of Polish, of English, is Vistula.
We crossed the Vistula.
Very heavy fighting was going on.
We crossed the Vistula.
We went again in a forest.
Little fighting was going on between in a few days, every few days, a little fighting, not very heavy fighting.
And one day, a officer from the, from the high officers come along.
He needed two men to be his messenger, messenger from the, from the command, from the high command.
We were, our regiment was standing like two rows.
And he was going between the two rows.
And he hold a little stick like a conductor from a orchestra.
He hold a little stick.
And he walk.
He walks slowly.
He look everyone in the face.
When he come in front of me, he hit me with the stick in my-- [INAUDIBLE] touch.
That's mean I to go out.
He pick me.
Pick me up and, and another Russian boy.
Same thing like I was look all right, was looking good.
They took us to headquarters.
They give us a lesson [? high ?] our duty will be to be a messenger, what we have to do.
After a few days, I was running to the headquarters with a message.
A sniper, a German sniper, was somewhere.
I didn't see him.
I don't know.
I will hear the bullets by my-- eight or 10 bullets was next to me, was shooting.
Chk.
I hear.
Chk.
Chk.
Chk.
And as you run, you don't run straight.
You run bent, like that.
You don't run straight, you run like that, zigzag to meet, to meet his eyes.
But he got me in my left arm.
He shot me.
Went to the-- I went to the place where they was, I was.
My regiment was already gone.
I can't see my regiment, already gone.
Because in the army, you're not-- in the front line, you don't stay on the place for weeks.
You go [? and start. ?] I already got another reg-- another people, another regiment.
As I was not shot in the, in the, in the legs or I be able to walk, you have to go yourself to the doctor.
They can't-- they wouldn't tie me on a, on a stretcher to carry me.
Because they have to be on the front line, too.
If I, if I can't walk, they do it.
But if I can walk, you do myself.
I went.
I find the, the ambulance where the, the, the doctors was.
They put me on a bandage.
Nighttime, I have the operation.
And what conditions?
In a tent, no light.
A tin from, from, from sardine with a bit of oil and a wick, this was the light where they make the operation, without anesthetic.
And the doctor told me, Ganc, tomorrow morning if you get temperature, we cut off the arm.
That's what he told me.
What can I do?
I can't do nothing, nothing.
They took me in a tent.
I was lying there on a tent on the ground and sleeping.
Next morning, I wake up.
I was so hungry, because two days, I was not eating, [? same what ?] I was shot until I got-- I got lost to my regiment.
And this, that I, I don't eat, or maybe one and a half or two days, I didn't eat nothing.
Was so hungry.
Next to me on the ground, another solider, a wounded soldier.
I can see he's a sick man.
He was red, temperature-- next to him, a piece of bread.
I ask him, can I have your bread, a piece of bread?
Take it.
Take it.
Take it.
He can't speak.
He's sick.
I took the bread, hard like a rock.
You can't bite it, hard like a rock.
Somebody brought me in a bit of water in a bucket.
I put the bread in the water, eat it.
In the nighttime, they took us to the hospital.
Where the hospital, because they are not allowed to travel with the trucks in the nighttime, because the bombs, they can drop bombs, the Germans, all in the nighttime.
They come in our place in the nighttime and lay down to sleep on the ground.
It was not bad, on the ground.
Straw covered with a little blanket.
And that's how we sleep.
Next morning, I'm up.
Got, I got, I got breakfast.
I told myself, I can walk.
I want go have a look where we are, where we are.
I walk on the, on the, I walk out to the yard.
And I see outside, gymnasium, a high school, a Polish high school.
The Polish government, when the German took over, they make a hospital.
When the German left, the Russian took over for hospital.
All right.
I told myself, I'll go have a look where I at-- Lublin, the town Lubin.
All right.
All right.
I know.
Let me say, I have wanted to see [? how it ?] is going on.
I went to the front gate.
I see on the other side from the, from the gate, on the other side from the fence.
Was not a fence from broads.
It was from, from pieces of iron, something we can see what's going on over the other side.
I see Polish people standing.
And they're discussing, like two parties, like the liberal, the labor, something like that.
He was like, he's right.
And he was like, he's right.
And I, and I see they are-- got papers.
They're selling the papers.
I can read Polish.
I can speak Polish.
I went close to the fence.
And I tell them on Polish, can you please give me a paper?
And I haven't got money.
Oh, they hear a Russian soldier speak Polish.
He give me the paper.
And he gave me the paper.
All right.
I went away.
I sit there and start to read the paper.
And I see one party are-- one party called [?
Akcja, ?] [?
Akcja, ?] [?
Akcja. ?] That's mean the [? army are ?] from the country.
This was, they was, they was going with the Germans against the Russians.
And the other party is [POLISH], army from the people's army.
They was going with the Russians against the Germans.
I start to read the paper.
I see that the [?
Akcja ?] killed people, Yiddish people, killed Red Army people, killed them.
They go into the hospital and take out.
If they find out a Yiddish in bed in a hospital, they take them out.
They kill them.
They take out the Russian soldiers.
They kill them.
Oh.
What is going on in Poland?
I didn't know.
Because I was in the army, I didn't know about it.
I, and I, when I was in the army anywhere, I always used to say, I am a Jew from Poland.
I was not afraid.
The soldiers that was lying there next to me in the hospital, they knew I am a Jew from Poland.
One day, this is not weeks, just same day, something like that, a lady comes in in the hospital, in the ward.
See, I was lying there.
And I see she take off the coat.
She put on a apron.
And she started to work, helping in the hospital, cleaning, this and that.
The, these Russian soldiers, they tell her that I am a Jew from Poland.
She come to me.
In Polish, she asked me from where I come, what town I was living in Poland.
From Chelm.
Chelm.
She just come from Chelm.
She was in Chelm.
She told me her story.
She is from Danzig, a town, in Polish, a town like Danzig.
Her husband was mobilized in the army, in the Polish army, in the first day, 1939.
She didn't hear nothing from him.
So she decided, she went back.
She went so far till she come to Chelm.
In Chelm, she stopped, because she doesn't want to go further to the Russians.
She stopped at Chelm.
And she told me what shop she got, a stationery shop.
The Germans give it to her.
Maybe she was a very good lady for the Germans.
The Germans give it to her, a stationery shop.
She told me the name from the shop, [POLISH].
I remember.
I remember.
She told me where she was living.
She was living in Doctor and [INAUDIBLE] building.
That doctor, that [INAUDIBLE], was a very rich man, a Polish.
He bought it, this building, a big building.
He bought this building before the war, a few months before the war.
And he kicked out all the Jewish people that were living in the house.
And he take in, took in Polish people.
So the Germans give it to her a flat in this house.
She was living there.
All right.
Finished the, the talk.
She left.
All right.
This woman, [INAUDIBLE], she went to told the [INAUDIBLE] hospital that I am a Jew from Poland.
At that time was a regulation in the Red Army.
If you are a Pole in Poland, you have to go to the Polish-- if you are in the Red, the Red Army, you have to go to the Polish army.
If you are born in Poland, you are in the Pol-- in the Red Army hospital, you have to go to Polish, Polish hospital.
After we have tea, the same day after we have tea, the [INAUDIBLE] comes along.
Ganc, [SPEAKING POLISH].
The [INAUDIBLE] Hospital want, wants to see me.
What can they wanted from me?
The [INAUDIBLE] Hospital.
What I've done?
Did I do wrong?
I did not anything wrong.
If the [INAUDIBLE] Hospital call you, you have to go.
I went to the [INAUDIBLE] Hospital.
In his office, he was sitting and doing his papers, the books as he looks.
I standing there about five, 10 minutes and he doesn't, doesn't say nothing.
Look like he forgot something or he's busy.
I was nervous.
And I said, [CLEARS THROAT], like that.
[CLEARS THROAT].
I wake him up.
Hey, I hear what a [CLEARS THROAT], like that.
He lift up his head.
He looked at me.
Hey, Ganc.
You already here.
All right, [? he ?] said.
He told me it's a order from the, from the high command, if you was born in Poland, you have to go back in Polish hospital.
That's mean if I go to the Polish hospital, as I was reading in the paper, if the Polish, The [?
Akcja ?] will find out I am there, they will take me out, and they kill me.
As I hear that, I stood up I salute to [SPEAKING RUSSIAN].
I will not go.
You can do with me what you wanted.
I will not go to the Polish hospital.
I was fighting in the Red Army.
I want to be in the Red Army Hospital He repeated.
[INAUDIBLE] I said.
That's mean you, you refuse to listen to the order.
Nyet.
[SPEAKING RUSSIAN].
I refuse.
All right.
Looked at me.
Go to sleep.
I didn't sleep.
I didn't sleep the night.
Was frightened maybe.
Next morning, they took me into theater, operating room.
They put me the arm in plaster.
And according to regulations, the army regulations, heavy wounded soldier is not allowed to be near, next to, not far from the front.
They have to be sent away.
Next morning, after put me plaster on, I was sent away to the [? deep ?] Ukraine [? area. ?] That's what saved my life.
And another, other story started.
All right.
Go on.
[DRINKS]
Pardon?
I went to my wife.
That was in 1945.
There.
Till we go back to Poland.
My, my son was born 1946.
I didn't have any.
This, this was the first child with born 1946, my first son.
Yeah.
In this little town, [RUSSIAN].
Yes, in '46.
In what month, I can't remember what month, we went back to Poland.
When I come back to Poland, we saw is not a place to live in Poland.
Because a pogrom was in Kielce, it killed 48 Yiddish people.
And the Polish people, the Polish people, if they saw somewhere a Yiddish man, they get [? a shell. ?] They kill him, stab him, beat him.
They won't [INAUDIBLE] him.
So I have a uncle and auntie in Australia and a cousin, which I used to have letters to him.
Matter of fact, I used to have a-- I have a permit in 1939 to come to Australia.
But my permit was not, was not good written.
The name, the name was not good.
I send them back.
And at that time was a, was a law.
If you want to come to Australia, you must have 40 [INAUDIBLE] in the bank.
This mean you, you will not, you will be able to carry on by yourself, don't have to be on security.
So I start to write letters.
And I, and I didn't get, get any answer.
But was my uncle that time have died.
And my cousin was in the army.
I [? didn't. ?] The president from the, from the Yiddish, Yiddish committee in Szczecin.
We were in Szczecin.
He was speaking English and writing English.
I give him 50 zloties.
He write a letter for me to the Victoria police.
And [? are ?] written, I ask him to send me the addresses for my friend, for my this, or from all of it.
I got-- in two weeks time, I got a letter back, a reply.
I got the letter here.
I would like to-- it is possible to.
Arrived, what?
I can't remember what month.
No, only the, only the [?
HIAS, ?] the Yiddish organization in America.
They helped me.
At '96, I got the permit, '46.
In 1948, we left Poland.
We come, come from, from-- I come to Paris from Chelm, from Szczecin.
We went to, to Paris.
And from Paris, we went to Belgium.
From Belgium, we come here with an [? airplane ?] [INAUDIBLE], like.
Oh, first stop, it was in-- first stop was in, in Cyprus.
I can't remember, but three or four stops.
At that time was not Fiji, was [INAUDIBLE].
Think that was the country was named, was a different name.
We was in Bombay, other places.
We didn't fly for long, or the non-stop.
In Sydney.
No, we don't remember what date.
In Sydney, we was only a few hours, because we straightaway, we catch a Australian airplane, TIA or something like that.
[INAUDIBLE], to Melbourne.
Nobody.
Nobody.
No.
Nobody.
Nobody.
He was in the army.
He was a single man.
He was in the army.
He was, he was wounded in the front.
He was wounded.
First two weeks, we was in the Relief Society.
Was a, a home.
We was there two week or one week.
We was in the Relief Society.
It then suddenly was a miracle.
Soon I left the Relief Society, I find work, same, same, same-- after a couple days, I find work.
And I already start to work.
I start to get paid, seven [INAUDIBLE] a week.
I, accidentally.
Somebody was, somebody told me, if you are a furrier, I know a furrier which he wants some people.
And he recommended me.
And he took me on [INAUDIBLE] right away I will start work.
Yeah, David Brothers.
